title: Masson’s Trichrome staining for histology
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"si04e","text":"Masson's trichrome is a histological staining method commonly used to distinguish collagen from muscle, cytoplasm and other tissue components.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"12mrc","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ch7sg","text":"The method presented here remains close to the original used by Masson in 1929.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4d118","text":"While there are many variants of the original method, there is commonality in that they all utilise three components - a nuclear stain (usually an iron haematoxylin), a fibre stain (to specifically dye the collagen) and a plasma stain (which acts as a counterstain). They also consistently use a polyacid to differentiate the plasma staining.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"bjuqq","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"coitr","text":"In this method it is noted that, if using routine formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded tissues, including the optional mordanting step will increase staining intensity and enhance contrast between tissue components. The same effect can also be achieved by using Bouin's fixative.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"5a28u","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"421vv","text":"Nuclear stain: This method uses Weigert's haematoxylin as the nuclear stain by staining the nuclei dark purple-black. Some variants use Verhoeff's haematoxylin to additionally highlight elastin by staining it black.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":14}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"8o8o","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"28s8","text":"Plasma stain: This method uses the original red dyes for the plasma stain, specifically a mixture of Acid fuchsin and Ponceau de xylidene. The latter has a number of alternate names, including Xylidine Ponceau, Xylidine Ponceau 2R, Ponceau 2R, Ponceau G, Ponceau Red, Acid Red 26. All have the same C.I. 16150.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":13}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"aset0","text":"Common variants use a mixture of Acid fuchsin and Biebrich scarlet, which provides a similar result, with a slightly darker red.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"9k8e4","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ga9h","text":"Heteropolyacid: Like the original, this method uses a 1% phosphomolybdic acid (PMA) solution for the heteropolyacid. Most variants use the same, but some recommend combining with phosphotungstic acid (PTA) in a 2.5% PMA + 2.5% PTA solution.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":15}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"3tb68","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"22v2a","text":"Fibre stain: The fibre stain used here is Methyl blue which stains collagen a vibrant, royal blue. This is similar to the orginal Masson's method, which was thought to use Aniline blue water soluble (a mixture of Water blue and Methyl blue). Some other variants use green fibre stains, such as Light green or Fast Green FCF. The Fast Green FCF resists fading better than Light green, which fades over time, causing sections to lose their contrast. One uncommon variant uses Metanil yellow to stain the collagen yellow.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":13}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"arn77","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"dbr2k","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: Dewax and rehydrate paraffin sections

Incubate in saturated picric acid solution at 57 °C Picric acid May & Baker LTD (CAS: 88-89-1) Saturated picric acid solution: 1.3 Mass Percent, in distilled water Picric acid is an unstable explosive when dry, where it becomes highly sensitive to shock, heat and friction. Ensure it remains hydrated at all times. Picric acid reacts with metals to form unstable picrate salts which are more explosive. Do not use metal containers, lids or racks. Picric acid is toxic if swallowed, inhaled or absorbed through the skin. Consult the MSDS and follow all recommended safety precautions.
Cool to Room temperature
Wash until no longer yellow

Stain in Weigert’s Haematoxylin (combine solution A & B immediately before use) Solution A: 1 g Haematoxylin in 100 ml of 95% Ethanol Solution B: 1.6 g Ferric chloride hexahydrate (Iron III chloride; FeCl 3 .6H 2 O) + 95 ml distilled water + 1 ml concentrated HCl Haematoxylin Fronine Laboratory Supplies Catalog #JL555S CI: 75290 Ethanol Hurst Scientific Catalog #EIMS95-5L (CAS: 64-17-5) Iron (III) chloride hexahydrate Merck MilliporeSigma (Sigma-Aldrich) Catalog #F2877 (CAS: 10025-77-1) Hydrochloric Acid Banksia Scientific Company (AJAX FineChem) Catalog #AJA256 (CAS: 7647-01-0)
Wash with running tap water to remove excess stain

Differentiate with acid alcohol (1-2 dips) to clear the slides [1% Hydrochloric Acid (HCl) in 70% Ethanol] Hydrochloric Acid Banksia Scientific Company (AJAX FineChem) Catalog #AJA256 (CAS: 7647-01-0) Ethanol POCD Scientific Catalog #ETHABS70WV5 (CAS: 64-17-5)
Wash in tap water

Stain in Acid Fuchsin + Ponceau de xylidine solution (make fresh, lasts 2-3 days) Solution: 1 g Acid Fuchsin + 1 g Ponceau de xylidine + 99 ml distilled water + 1 ml Glacial acetic acid Acid Fuchsin Fronine Laboratory Supplies Catalog #JL301 CI: 42685 Ponceau de Xylidene E. Gurr Ltd CI: 16150 Glacial acetic acid Ajax Finechem (ThermoFisher Scientific) Catalog #AJA1-500ML (CAS: 64-19-7)
Wash in distilled water

Differentiate and mordant in 1% PMA solution 2 – 6 mins (check progress of differentiation under the microscope every 2 mins, until collagen is colourless) PMA Solution: 1 g Phosphomolybdic acid + 100 ml distilled water Phosphomolybdic acid Merck MilliporeSigma (Sigma-Aldrich) Catalog #221856 (CAS: 51429-74-4)
Drain – do not rinse

Stain in Methyl blue solution Solution: 2 g Methyl blue + 2.5 ml Glacial acetic acid + 100 ml distilled water (solution can be re-used, lasts many years) Methyl Blue Merck MilliporeSigma (Sigma-Aldrich) Catalog #M5528 (CAS: 28983-56-4) CI: 42780 Glacial acetic acid Ajax Finechem (ThermoFisher Scientific) Catalog #AJA1-500ML (CAS: 64-19-7)
Wash in distilled water
Differentiate with 1% Glacial acetic acid Glacial acetic acid Ajax Finechem (ThermoFisher Scientific) Catalog #AJA1-500ML (CAS: 64-19-7)
95% ethanol Ethanol Hurst Scientific Catalog #EIMS95-5L (CAS: 64-17-5)

100% ethanol Ethanol POCD Scientific Catalog #ETHABS20M (CAS: 64-17-5) Note: ensure whole slide and slide rack is fully immersed in ethanol to remove all traces of water, preventing water contamination of the xylene

100% ethanol Ethanol POCD Scientific Catalog #ETHABS20M (CAS: 64-17-5) Note: ensure whole slide and slide rack is fully immersed in ethanol to remove all traces of water, preventing water contamination of the xylene
100% Xylene Xylene POCD Scientific Catalog #XYL5P (CAS: 64-17-5)
100% Xylene Xylene POCD Scientific Catalog #XYL5P (CAS: 64-17-5)
Coverslip with a resinous mounting medium, such as DPX DPX Mountant for histology Merck MilliporeSigma (Sigma-Aldrich) Catalog #06522 (CAS: 130-12-2)

Examine slides under microscope to assess nuclear staining. Nuclei should be dark purple-black and cytoplasm or collagenous tissue components should be grey. If nuclei are under-stained, return to step 5. If sections are over-stained, return to step 7.
Nuclei: Purple-black Cytoplasm: Red Keratin: Red Erythrocytes: Red Muscle: Red Collagen: Blue
creator: Samson Dowland
